intent: NEGATIVE FIXTURE: an RBAC/role constraint denial (newrole_t self dyntransition, role staff_r -> sysadm_r). The TE allow exists, so the role constraint is the sole binding reason. A raw TE allow cannot fix it. RuleSteward must DETECT-and-DECLINE. Distinct from scenario 39 by using a different role pair (staff_r -> sysadm_r vs staff_r -> system_r).
_kinds_note: Here BOTH layers agree: authoritative=Constraint (role constrain DENIED via replay), floor=RoleSuspected (scontext role staff_r vs tcontext role sysadm_r mismatch, record-detectable). On the targeted policy RBAC collapses into the Constraint bucket; there is no distinct RBAC string (f4 section 8).

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow newrole_t self:process dyntransition;  (BOGUS - the role constraint blocks it regardless of the TE allow, which already exists)
oracle.audit2allow_R: same BOGUS allow newrole_t self:process dyntransition; plus the constraint comment block
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: N/A - decline scenario. expected.te is rule-free and compiles exit 0.
oracle.audit2why_detail: constrain process { transition noatsecure siginh rlimitinh dyntransition } ((r1 == r2 -Fail-) or ... (t1 == can_system_change -Fail-) and (r2 == system_r -Fail-) or (t1 == process_uncond_exempt -Fail-)); Constraint DENIED. Possible cause is the source role (staff_r) and target role (sysadm_r) are different.

triage_expectation.should_decline_with_reason: RBAC/role constraint violation: source role staff_r differs from target role sysadm_r on a process dyntransition. A TE allow cannot fix a role constraint; the fix is an RBAC change (role_allow / authorize the role for the type).
triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive impl emits the BOGUS allow newrole_t self:process dyntransition; (audit2allow does exactly this). The TE allow already EXISTS, so adding it again changes nothing - the role constraint still blocks. The floor's RoleSuspected flag (role mismatch) catches it offline.
